feature.name: Focusgroup
feature.summary: Declarative HTML attribute giving composite widgets arrow-key navigation, a guaranteed tab stop, and last-focused memory, replacing hand-coded roving tabindex scripts.
feature.creator: travil@microsoft.com
feature.standardsMaturity: Working draft (WHATWG HTML PR #11723, under development)

intake.finding: Feature is a deliberate re-scope/revival of an earlier (2022-01-11, travil@microsoft.com) broader focusgroup proposal. The developer problem - hand-coded roving tabindex for composite widgets - is real, recurring, and independently re-implemented by React, Angular CDK, and Fluent UI per the RFC blog. The re-scoping (enumerated behavior tokens, deferred CSS+grid, move toward WHATWG) directly responded to the earlier round's venue and scope criticism, which the RFC blog frames as 'bringing focusgroup back.' Microsoft-authored with Google (Mason Freed) owning the Chromium origin trial - a cross-org launch.
intake.limitations: Original intake artifacts (problem brief, segment map) are not separately published; the explainer subsumes them.

incubation.finding: Genuine multi-venue incubation: OpenUI Community Group (primary venue), ARIA Working Group (w3c/aria#1947 exploring 'focusgroups on elements that aren't expected'), W3C APA (ACTION-2326 report on the proposal), and an earlier W3C TAG early review (#732). The scoped explainer is comprehensive: goals/non-goals, alternatives considered, privacy/security sections, open questions, and polyfilling. The move to a WHATWG HTML PR resolved the 'concerns: venue' raised against the original OpenUI-only version.
incubation.limitations: No published developer-research evidence ledger (interviews, demand surveys) beyond the roving-tabindex boilerplate argument; demand is argued from framework practice rather than measured proposal-specific demand.

prototype.finding: Chromium implementation exists behind the Focusgroup flag (Blink>DOM) with task-based interactive demos and the RFC blog (2026-03-05) inviting feedback. The spec PR history shows rapid iteration: nomemory token, default modifiers per behavior, <button> child role inference, top-layer exclusion, key-conflict and scrolling-interaction handling. DevTools error logging for invalid focusgroup value combinations is supported.
prototype.limitations: No public friction-log or framework/server/mobile compatibility-matrix artifact was found; prototype evidence is demo-led, and the iOS platform is explicitly not targeted.

developerTrials.finding: M146 behind-a-flag developer trial plus a public RFC (2026-03-05) with explicit feedback questions, a GitHub issue route (openui/open-ui), and a polyfill enabling pre-availability testing. Two independent developer analyses exist (Steve Frenzel, 2026-04-20; Eric Eggert's is older). The cohort, task-completion rates, and methodology were not published.
developerTrials.limitations: No public cohort representativeness report, task-completion data, or structured-survey results; feedback is anecdotal via issues and blog posts. Eggert's critique predates the scoped rewrite.

wideReview.finding: Multi-stakeholder review is real but unfinished at ship. Mozilla position #1348 is Positive (opened 2026-02-03). W3C TAG #1152 (Incubation: Scoped Focusgroup, opened 2025-09-18) is still Open with 'Progress: pending external feedback' across API design, accessibility, and web architecture. WebKit #171 remains Open / 'Needs position' with original concerns:venue + accessibility (cookiecrook/J. Craig on decoupling focus/selection and AT changes). Per the cached record, tag_review_status, security_review_status, and privacy_review_status were all 'Pending' when the feature shipped in M150.
wideReview.limitations: The feature shipped a new web-facing HTML attribute while TAG, security, and privacy reviews were still Pending and WebKit had no resolved positive position; 'No signal' from WebKit in the cached record is a flagged risk, not neutral.

experiment.finding: An Origin Trial (ID 4231694799868002305) ran desktop+android from M146 through M149 with ot_setup_status=complete and goals of testing the new design and gathering feedback. However the cached record carries no experiment_risks, no origin_trial_feedback_url, and no published readout; no public participant denominator, hypotheses, metrics, or findings were located.
experiment.limitations: The OT intent thread (Google Groups) could not be retrieved by the reader and no interim/final readout is public; it is unknown what the OT measured or decided, so the experiment phase cannot be scored as supported.

prepareToShip.finding: Intent to Ship thread exists (stage 160). WHATWG spec PR #11723 marked ready-for-review 2025-11-24 and iterated through 2026-04. Docs/demos are strong (RFC blog, explainer, interactive demos, polyfill). adoption_expectation cites Fluent UI Web Components V3 integration 'expected to ship in the coming months' - a commitment, not verified production deployment. MDN and caniuse entries are absent (Mozilla issue records 'MDN URL: No response', 'Caniuse URL: No response').
prepareToShip.limitations: Partner adoption is interest/commitment-stage only; MDN/caniuse/Baseline reference docs are not yet in place; availability_expectation honestly states Baseline depends on other implementations.

release.finding: Chrome 150 stable (2026-06-30) shipped Focusgroup for Android, ChromeOS, Linux, macOS, and Windows (desktop/android/webview 150; iOS null). Coordinated comms: Chrome for Developers RFC blog, Chrome 150 beta blog, release notes, and a LinkedIn announcement. Release-notes and beta-blog descriptions match the shipped Chrome-only behaviour and the keyboard/arrows requirement; no deprecations accompanied it. General press (Winaero, X/AlternativeTo) coverage was accurate and non-hyped.
release.limitations: Release communications do not prominently surface that TAG/security/privacy reviews are Pending or that no second engine has shipped; the limitation is acknowledged only indirectly via availability_expectation.

adoption.finding: The Focusgroup counter (bucket 4243) is a web-feature-family counter (scope: web-feature-family; mapping confidence: exact - ChromeStatus web_feature 'focusgroup' maps one-to-one to counter 'Focusgroup'), so it may cover more behavior than this individual launch and is not exact feature adoption. It reports the share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads where the counter fired at least once. First positive reading: 0.0001% on 2022-08-05 (earlier broad proposal). At the M146 dev-trial/origin-trial stable date (2026-03-10): 0.000026%; day-30 (2026-04-09): 0.000022%; day-90 (2026-06-08, ~3 weeks before M150 ship): 0.0026% - a clear pre-ship uptick from near-zero. At the M150 ship stable date (2026-06-30): 0.0156%, having peaked at 0.0163% on 2026-06-29 (the day before stable); latest observed point: 0.0108% (2026-07-21, ~3 weeks post-ship). M150 day-30/90/180 checkpoints are not yet available. Fluent UI Web Components V3 integration remains intended (commitment, not verified) and the @microsoft/focusgroup-polyfill has 1 dependent. With no predeclared usage target and no verified production/retention evidence, the adoption phase is described, not scored.
adoption.limitations: The counter is page-load share, not unique developers/sites/users, successful tasks, satisfaction, production deployment, or end-user benefit; a web-feature-family counter may cover more behavior than this launch, so it is not exact feature adoption. No predeclared usage target exists to interpret the low level; no production deployment, case study, or retention evidence corroborates it; low usage is not scored as failure without a target.

support.finding: Support route is the OpenUI GitHub issue tracker; recurring themes are visible (interactions with native elements - openui#995; focusgroup on unexpected elements - w3c/aria#1947). No public support-health, recurrence, or triage dashboard exists. Feature shipped 2026-06-30, only weeks before the evidence cutoff.
support.limitations: No support-burden, recurrence, or resolution-time data; too early post-ship to assess support outcomes.

deprecation.finding: Not applicable: this is a new-feature launch (a new HTML attribute), not a deprecation or removal. There is no existing API to remove, no migration path to define, and no deprecation event in the manifest. Raised for completeness per the schema requirement.
deprecation.limitations: None; phase is genuinely out of scope for this feature type.

developerValue.finding: The developer problem is well-validated: roving tabindex for toolbars/tablists/menus/listboxes/radiogroups is universal boilerplate, independently re-implemented across React, Angular CDK, and Fluent UI, and the declarative one-attribute solution is widely welcomed by independent developers (Frenzel: 'super stoked... high hopes').
developerValue.limitations: Demand is argued from framework practice rather than proposal-specific survey data.

endUserImpact.finding: Potential positive end-user impact (standardized arrow navigation, guaranteed single tab stop, last-focused memory, APG-aligned role inference) is plausible but unmeasured. Independent accessibility experts raised real misuse risks: Eggert warned that generalising arrow navigation invites over-eager misuse analogous to tabindex/ARIA abuse, and Frenzel flagged unclear menu-vs-navigation distinction and missing JAWS/NVDA feedback in the chatbot pattern. No production user-impact data exists; the available Chrome telemetry is page-load usage, not end-user or AT-user benefit.
endUserImpact.limitations: No measured end-user or assistive-technology user impact; risk of developer misuse is expert-judged, not observed at scale. Page-load share is not end-user-benefit evidence.

adoption.finding: Measured Chrome usage now exists but remains unscored. The Focusgroup web-feature-family counter (bucket 4243; exact one-to-one mapping from ChromeStatus web_feature 'focusgroup') fired on 0.0156% of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads at the M150 ship date (2026-06-30), up from 0.0026% at the M146 day-90 checkpoint (2026-06-08) and ~0.000026% at OT start (2026-03-10); it peaked at 0.0163% (2026-06-29) and reads 0.0108% at the latest point (2026-07-21). This is the share of page loads where the counter fired - not unique developers, sites, users, successful tasks, or verified production deployment. Fluent UI V3 integration is still commitment-stage and the polyfill has 1 dependent. With no predeclared usage target and no production/retention corroboration, the low measured level cannot be interpreted as success or failure; it is described, not scored.
adoption.limitations: No predeclared usage target/threshold; the web-feature-family counter is page-load share (not unique-site/developer/user, production, retention, task-success, or user-benefit evidence) and may cover more behavior than this launch. Low usage is not scored as failure without a target; measured usage is not scored as success without production evidence.

interoperability.finding: The headline risk. Shipped Chrome-only (Chrome/Edge/Chromium) in M150; Baseline is blocked because no second engine has implemented. Mozilla is Positive (#1348) but has announced no implementation. WebKit remains 'Needs position' (original venue + accessibility concerns; the cached availability_expectation says testability concerns were 'largely addressed' but there is no resolved positive position). TAG review is still pending. WHATWG spec is an open Working-Draft PR. Genuinely cross-venue incubated, but no second implementation or confirmed Baseline path.
interoperability.limitations: wpt.fyi dashboard is JS-only and could not be read; cross-engine WPT conformance not independently confirmed. 'No signal' from WebKit is treated as risk, not neutrality.

implementationQuality.finding: Spec is detailed and actively iterated (top-layer handling, key-conflict escape behaviour, scrolling interactions, default modifiers, role-inference precedence), WPT is referenced (cached wpt=true), and DevTools surfaces invalid focusgroup value combinations. The polyfill documents known divergences and limitations (no focusGroup property, reading-flow unsupported, visibility-change gaps).
implementationQuality.limitations: Implementation quality is Chromium-centric; cross-engine conformance and regression coverage are not independently evidenced; polyfill notes the spec is 'still evolving.'

evidenceAndCommunication.finding: External communication was accurate and transparent (RFC blog, release notes, beta blog match the Chrome-only shipped reality; availability_expectation honestly states Baseline depends on other implementations). However the internal evidence base for the ship decision is weak in public: the OT ran with no recorded risks, no feedback URL, and no published readout, and the cached record shows TAG/security/privacy reviews all Pending at ship. Intent threads are not machine-readable.
evidenceAndCommunication.limitations: Could not retrieve intent-thread contents (Google Groups); OT findings are not public, so the ship-decision evidence cannot be independently audited.

support.finding: Public issue route and recurring design themes exist but no support-health dashboard, recurrence/resolution data, or post-ship incident record; too early to assess.
support.limitations: No support metrics; feature shipped weeks before cutoff.

overall.finding: A well-motivated, genuinely cross-venue-incubated accessibility primitive that responded to prior criticism by re-scoping, with accurate release communication and a positive Mozilla position. But it shipped Chrome-only with WebKit unresolved and TAG/security/privacy reviews Pending, no second implementation or confirmed Baseline path, an Origin Trial that left no public readout, adoption now measurable but low (Focusgroup counter fired on 0.0156% of Chrome page loads at the M150 ship date and 0.0108% at the latest point, 2026-07-21) yet unscored for lack of a predeclared target and production corroboration, and unresolved expert concerns about developer misuse. Net: a credible start with material interop, evidence, and adoption risks still open.
overall.limitations: Overall is mixed, not success or failure; adoption now has measured page-load telemetry (low) but remains unscored without a target or production evidence, and support and end-user impact remain unscored for insufficient evidence.

supports: Feature metadata, owners, stages, OT ID/use counter, standards maturity, Mozilla/WebKit/web-dev views, availability/adoption expectations
Launch milestones (dev trial M146, OT M146-M149, ship M150), review statuses Pending
sources.limitations: ChromeStatus UI is JS-rendered; the authoritative cached JSON (accurate_as_of 2026-06-26) was used. tag_review_status/security_review_status/privacy_review_status shown as Pending.

sources.publisher: ChromeStatus feature-popularity timeline (cached; Chromium UseCounter UMA)
supports: Focusgroup web-feature-family counter (bucket 4243; ChromeStatus web_feature 'focusgroup' maps one-to-one to counter 'Focusgroup', confidence exact): share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads where the counter fired >=1x

Launch-relative checkpoints - M146 dev-trial/OT stable 2026-03-10: 0.000026%; day-30 (2026-04-09): 0.000022%; day-90 (2026-06-08): 0.0026%; M150 enabled-by-default stable 2026-06-30: 0.0156% (M150 day-30/90/180 not yet available)
Trajectory: first positive 2022-08-05 (0.0001%, earlier broad proposal); peak 2026-06-29 (0.0163%); latest 2026-07-21 (0.0108%); 1477 data points
sources.limitations: Value is the share of measured Chrome page loads where the counter fired - NOT unique developers, sites, users, successful tasks, satisfaction, production deployment, or end-user benefit. Scope is web-feature-family (may cover more behavior than this ChromeStatus launch), so it is not exact feature adoption though the feature-to-counter mapping is one-to-one (confidence exact). UMA reflects participating Chrome clients and the dominant milestone; enterprise/opt-out populations may be underrepresented; zero/missing/delayed points are not proof of zero real-world use. Timeline cached 2026-07-22 (SHA-256 edada440267415ad0163ea7691650da3045a46ad9d8020a04484f6dfdd8d7ea7).

supports: Independent accessibility-expert critique: generalising arrow navigation risks over-eager misuse; prefers ARIA-role-gated default keyboard behaviour; dislikes tabindex=-1 making elements unreachable in non-supporting browsers; HTML+CSS conflict concern

successes: Problem validity is strongly established and independently corroborated by framework practice across React, Angular CDK, and Fluent UI; the declarative one-attribute solution was welcomed by independent developers.
Genuine, multi-venue incubation (OpenUI, ARIA WG, W3C APA, W3C TAG, Mozilla, WebKit) with a comprehensive explainer including privacy/security/alternatives/open questions.

The team correctly responded to the earlier round's criticism by re-scoping (enumerated behavior tokens, deferred CSS and grid, move to a WHATWG PR), resolving the original WebKit 'concerns: venue' objection.
Release communication (RFC blog, Chrome 150 beta blog, release notes, LinkedIn) was accurate, multi-channel, and matched the Chrome-only shipped behaviour; no hype or overclaiming.
Mozilla delivered a Positive standards position (#1348) and a maintained polyfill plus interactive demos lowered the barrier to early evaluation.
failures: Shipped Chrome-only in M150 while TAG, security, and privacy reviews were all still Pending, with no accepted-risk note recorded.

Origin Trial (M146-M149) left no public readout: no experiment_risks, no feedback_url, no hypotheses/metrics/participant denominator or findings - the ship-decision evidence is not independently auditable.
No second engine has implemented; Baseline is blocked and WebKit remains 'Needs position', so the feature launches with an unconfirmed cross-engine path for a new web-facing HTML attribute.

Adoption evidence is commitment-stage only (Fluent UI V3 'expected'); the polyfill has just 1 dependent and no verified production deployments or case studies exist. The Focusgroup web-feature-family counter now shows measurable but low Chrome page-load share (0.0156% at the M150 ship date, 0.0108% as of 2026-07-21), but with no predeclared usage target the level cannot be contextualised.
Reference/docs gaps: no MDN or caniuse entry at launch; intent threads are not machine-readable.
counterfactuals: Had the team published an OT readout (hypotheses, participant denominator, measured outcomes, contradictions), the M150 ship decision and any late spec refinements would be publicly defensible instead of unscored.
Had TAG/security/privacy reviews been resolved (or an explicit accepted-risk recorded) before M150 ship, the Chrome-only launch of a new web-facing attribute would carry materially less review risk.
Had WebKit's accessibility/venue position been formally updated to positive before ship, the Baseline path would be credible; instead the feature ships with no confirmed second implementation.

The earlier (2022-23) broad proposal would likely have stayed blocked on venue had the team not re-scoped to enumerated behavior tokens and moved incubation to WHATWG - the re-scope was load-bearing for the revival.

A use-counter readout is now available (Focusgroup fired on 0.0156% of Chrome page loads at the M150 ship date, 0.0108% at the latest point), yet adoption remains unscored because there is no predeclared usage target and no verified production/retention evidence to interpret the low level; had a target been declared at launch, the measured page-load share could have been scored.
skillImprovements: Add a phase-05 (Experiment) eval gate: an Origin Trial may only be marked 'supported' when a readout (hypotheses, participant denominator, measured outcomes, limitations) is linked or explicitly marked missing; forbid marking experiment 'supported' on setup-status alone.

Add a wide-review/ship gate check that flags any feature shipping while tag_review_status, security_review_status, or privacy_review_status are 'Pending', requiring an explicit accepted-risk note before the release phase can be 'supported'.

Add an interop/Baseline regression eval for new web-facing attributes: require current Mozilla and WebKit position URLs with dates and a Baseline-path statement; treat 'No signal' as a flagged risk, never as neutral/positive.

Strengthen the adoption-evidence-ladder check to (a) distinguish 'expected framework integration' from 'verified production deployment' and forbid promoting commitment to deployment without an attributable source, and (b) require a predeclared usage target/threshold before a use-counter readout (page-load share) can score adoption - a counter alone, without a target or production/retention evidence, leaves adoption unscored (as demonstrated by Focusgroup, where measured telemetry exists but no target leaves the outcome unscored).

Add a source-retrievability rule: capture Google Groups intent-thread content and JS-only dashboards (wpt.fyi, chromestatus.com) into the cached evidence or require a server-rendered fallback (e.g., chromestatuslite) before counting a phase as evidenced.

Add a misuse-risk lens for accessibility primitives: when independent a11y experts raise over-eager-misuse concerns, require a documented authoring-guidance/escape plan before scoring end-user impact as success.

missingEvidence: Origin Trial readout: hypotheses, participant denominator, measured outcomes, and findings are not public (no experiment_risks, no origin_trial_feedback_url in the cached record; no published readout located).
Intent-to-Ship and Intent-to-Experiment thread contents could not be retrieved (Google Groups URLs failed the reader); ship rationale not independently auditable.

Usage telemetry scope: the Focusgroup web-feature-family counter (bucket 4243) gives Chrome page-load share only - not unique-site, unique-developer, or unique-user counts, and a family counter may cover more behavior than this individual launch; no HTTP Archive/web-features adoption counts or Chrome UX evidence located.
Predeclared usage target: no launch-time usage target/threshold exists to interpret the measured low page-load share, so adoption remains unscored despite available telemetry.
Verified production adoption: no case studies or confirmed deployments beyond Fluent UI V3 intent (commitment-stage); no retention/task-success/user-benefit evidence.
Cross-engine WPT conformance: wpt.fyi dashboard is JS-only and returned no readable per-engine results.
Other-engine implementation: no Firefox or Safari implementation; Baseline blocked - confirmed indirectly, not via a direct web-features row read.
MDN/caniuse reference entries absent at launch.
